text: The surgeon separates the left lobe of the liver using a Nathanson liver retractor. A Buzzi, thirty-six French instrument is visible. The surgeon begins dissection of the short gastric vessels at the level of the second gastric pedicle, applying anteromedial traction to the anterior wall of the stomach with the left hand, while the assistant holds the short gastric vessels with the right hand.

The surgeon uses a harmonic or ligature device to separate approximately twelve millimeters of the short gastric vessels near the fundus, preparing the stomach for a floppy fundus wrap during the laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication procedure.
The assistant rearranges the grasper to hold the posterior attachment of the short gastric, which is being separated.
The surgeon continues the dissection, progressing towards the left crus of the diaphragm, reaching the upper level of the surgical field.

The surgeon divides a piece of omentum and mobilizes the stomach from the superior pole of the spleen at the level of the left crus. Adhesions between the stomach and spleen are separated using anteromedial traction.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the stomach, finishing the adhesiolysis on the left side. Traction is applied to begin the anterior mobilization of the esophagus, starting from either the pars placida or the angular face, reaching up to the left crus.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the gastric fundus. The pars flaccida is visible, and anterolateral traction is applied to open the peritoneum over the pars flaccida, allowing for further dissection.

The surgeon identifies the caudate lobe and the vena cava located just below it, exercising caution to avoid contact with the caudate lobe during crural approximation. The stomach is pulled downward to facilitate the separation of the anterior peritoneum over the esophagus, ensuring the anterior vagus nerve remains unharmed by maintaining tension on the stomach.

The surgeon addresses minor bleeding near the caudate lobe, ensuring not to extend posteriorly. Anterolateral traction is applied to open the anterior peritoneum over the esophagus, revealing the diaphragmatic crura.

The surgeon separates the right crus from the esophagus, using suction irrigation to provide anterior traction. A blunt grasper is used to create a window posterior to the esophagus, moving from right to left. The peritoneum over the left crus is separated, forming a buttonhole posterior to the esophagus. The suction irrigation is positioned on the right side, while the grasper is on the left side.

The surgeon completes the posterior mobilization of the esophagus, forming a posterior window to facilitate the wrap. The window is visible and is being enlarged to ensure adequate space for the procedure.
The surgeon completes the posterior window formation and enlarges it using blunt dissection, creating enough space to accommodate the gastric wrap.
The surgeon uses a grasper to take a bite for a crural approximation, applying a mattress suture in a figure-of-eight pattern.
The surgeon takes a reverse bite suture from the right crus, inserting the needle from outside to inside, then continues the suture from inside to outside on the left crus.
The surgeon takes a bite from right to left, followed by another bite from left to right, creating a mattress suture pattern. The suture is placed one by one for precision.
The surgeon closes the suture using an intracorporeal knot, ensuring a good approximation that allows a five-millimeter instrument to pass between the crura and the esophagus.

The surgeon ties a Sargent's knot with a configuration of two, one, one, starting with a double wrap followed by two opposite alternating wraps. The crural approximation is adjusted to allow a 5mm instrument to pass through, ensuring it is neither too tight nor too loose.
The surgeon completes the crural approximation, securing the sutures around the crura to achieve the desired tension and closure.
The surgeon trims the tail suture and removes the needle, then performs the fundus pull by grasping the fundus and pulling it out to create the wrap.

The surgeon pulls the fundus wrap, ensuring the posterior valve is pulled more to avoid torsion of the esophagus. The 'buttock sign' is visible, indicating equal positioning of the wrap, which aids in proper fixation.

The surgeon completes the fixation of the fundoplication and proceeds with the final step of the surgery, performing a stomach-to-stomach plication. The stomach is approximated to itself with two or three sutures, forming a wrap.
The surgeon ties a tumble square knot or surgeon's knot to secure the fundoplication wrap, ensuring the stomach is properly approximated.

The surgeon ties the first knot using a surgeon's knot technique, starting with a double wrap followed by two alternating single wraps. The second knot is placed approximately two centimeters below, securing the stomach to stomach wrap.
The surgeon holds the suture and forms a 'C' shape, shortening the tail before performing a double wrap knot.

The surgeon ties the second knot using a two-one-one technique with a double wrap followed by two alternating single wraps, ensuring the knot is tightened securely. The suture tail is positioned to one side, using non-absorbable polyester material such as Ethibond or Dacron. After securing the knot, the suture is cut and the needle is removed.
